origin: A short Greek genealogical line from the biblical tradition, most likely the genealogy in the Gospel of Matthew (showing the Davidic/Judean royal line: Hezekiah → Manasseh → Amon → Josiah). The wording resembles Matthew 1:10; the form Ἀμὼς appears like a copying/orthographic slip for the expected Ἀμὼν (Amon).
author: Most likely the author/compiler traditionally associated with the Gospel of Matthew (i.e., “Matthew” as understood in early Christian tradition), or more generally a Greek-scripture scribe/translator preserving Matthew’s text.
thoughts: The author’s perspective is doctrinal and legitimizing: the genealogy is included to confirm that the promised Messiah fits the expected line (Davidic descent) and that sacred history forms a coherent fulfillment of God’s plan. The author likely intends the reader to see continuity from the respected kings of Judah to the later figure at the center of the Gospel.
audience: Early Jewish or Jewish-Christian readers (and secondarily Greek-speaking Christians) who are interested in Jesus’ identity and messianic credentials—particularly readers who value lineage, Scripture fulfillment, and royal/Davidic legitimacy.
written_when: Late 1st century CE (roughly 80–90 CE is a common scholarly estimate for Matthew’s final form). The Greek language also fits this period of manuscript transmission; it could have been copied/transmitted later as well, but the underlying composition is typically dated to that late 1st-century window.
